coredata.dc:title: Sensitive determination of naftazone using carbon quantum dots nanoprobe by fluorimetry and smartphone-based techniques
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Khateeb, Lateefa A.
El-Maghrabey, Mahmoud
El-Shaheny, Rania
coredata.dc:description: A simple, fast, and direct mix-and-read spectrofluorimetric method has been developed for the sensitive determination of naftazone (NFZ) utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a greener and highly luminescent nanosensor. NFZ effectively quenches the strong fluorescence of GQDs at λex/λem of 350/440 nm via the inner filter effect mechanism. The nanosensor exhibits excellent linearity for NFZ over the concentration range of 0.46 to 186 μM with a limit of detection of 0.04 μM. The proposed method was validated for the successful determination of NFZ in tablets and on manufacturing equipment surfaces with good % recoveries of 98.4–101.6 and 96.3 – 102.2%, respectively. Furthermore, an integrated smartphone-based reader has been implemented and successfully applied for the determination of NFZ. The smartphone-based reader consists of a 365 nm UV torch as an excitation source, a smartphone for recording images, and smartphone-powered image analysis software for signal interpretation, together with a paper-based analytical device (PAD) utilizing filter paper as a substrate and correction fluid as a barrier for creation of detection zones. This smart platform showed excellent sensitivity with a limit of detection down to 0.12 nmol/zone, and it could be used for in-site determination of NFZ, especially for the limited resources laboratories.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Naftazone
Fluorimetry
Graphene quantum dots
Smartphone-based reader
Inner filter effect
Paper-based analytical device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Technique for the determination of drug remainders on apparatus surface
Paper-based device construction and testing its linearity for NFZ determination by image processing.
Principle of the fluorescence sensor and detection mechanism
Performance of the nanoprobe for determination of NFZ
Determination of drug residues on manufacturing device surfaces
Development and application of a smartphone-based reader and paper-based analytical device for rapid determination of NFZ
Comparison of the developed method with the reported analytical literature
Examination of methods’ greenness and eco-impact

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research (DSR), King Abdulaziz University , Jeddah, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, under grant No. (G: 506-247-1442 ). The authors, therefore, acknowledge with thanks DSR for the financial support provided.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Principle of the detection mechanism of NFZ by GQDs, and (b) illustration of primary and secondary IFE with a top view of the cuvette showing the incident (I0), transmitted (I), and the pathway for detecting fluorescence (F) at 90°, the pIFE is shown as yellow star and the sIFE is shown as a red star.
Schematic illustration of the implemented smartphone-based device for NFZ determination.

Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs alone and after the addition of rising concentrations of NFZ; the insets are photos of GQDs under 365 nm UV radiation in the absence (1) and the presence (2) of NFZ.

(a) GQDs excitation and emission spectra, and NFZ absorption spectrum; displaying significant overlying, (b) repressed efficacy (%E) of the observed and corrected fluorescence of GQDs after adding increasing concentrations of NFZ, (c) Stern-Volmer’s plots for GQDs-NFZ at various temperatures, and (d) absorption spectra of GQDs utilizing water as a reference, and GQDs-NFZ using NFZ as a reference.

(a) Plots of fluorescence ratio F0/F versus NFZ concentrations while the inset shows the linearity fitting over narrow concentration ranges of the corresponding curves, and (b) shows the Plot of LnF0/F versus concentrations of NFZ.
Illustration of the procedure adopted for residual drug determination on the equipment surface.

The results of the analysis of NFZ by the developed smartphone reader, where (a) the results of the test for uniform illumination of the GQDs in the paper-based chip indicator zones, (b) an image of the different concentrations of NFZ (0–30 nmol/zone) added to the indicator zones containing GQDs, and (c) calibration graph for NFZ plotted by applying Green channel value calculated by RGB detector software.

The graph and analytical greenness score obtained by AGREE software for the proposed (a) fluorimetry and (b) smartphone-based methods, and the annotated result of the generic assessment with the span of the color map used in the graph and the corresponding values.
Validation data for determination of NFZ by the developed fluorimetry method.

Drug solution stability (fresh and 7-day aged solutions)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *y = Ln (F0/F) where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of blank and samples, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the determination of NFZ in tablet using the proposed spectrofluorimetric method and its comparison o with those of the reference HPLC method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each result is the average of three separate determinations.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values between parentheses are the tabulated t and F values, respectively, at P = 0.05 [39].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the recovery study of NFZ from spiked stainless-steel surface swabbed with cotton wick.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of the analysis of NFZ by the developed smartphone reader.
Comparison of the performance of the developed method with those reported in the literature for the determination of NFZ.

row.entry: measurements at 520 nm after spraying lead acetate

Mobile phase: 0.02 M NaH2PO4 in 60% aqueous methanol
Stationary phase: phenyl column
Mobile phase: Gradient elution of 0.1% formic acid/10 mM ammonium formate and acetonitrile. Stationary phase: OSD column
- PAD, GQDs as fluorescent sensor, and smartphone-reader as a dectetor

head.ce:title: Sensitive determination of naftazone using carbon quantum dots nanoprobe by fluorimetry and smartphone-based techniques

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, King Abdulaziz University, P.O Box 80200-Jeddah 21589, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, fast, and direct mix-and-read spectrofluorimetric method has been developed for the sensitive determination of naftazone (NFZ) utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a greener and highly luminescent nanosensor. NFZ effectively quenches the strong fluorescence of GQDs at λex/λem of 350/440 nm via the inner filter effect mechanism. The nanosensor exhibits excellent linearity for NFZ over the concentration range of 0.46 to 186 μM with a limit of detection of 0.04 μM. The proposed method was validated for the successful determination of NFZ in tablets and on manufacturing equipment surfaces with good % recoveries of 98.4–101.6 and 96.3 – 102.2%, respectively. Furthermore, an integrated smartphone-based reader has been implemented and successfully applied for the determination of NFZ. The smartphone-based reader consists of a 365 nm UV torch as an excitation source, a smartphone for recording images, and smartphone-powered image analysis software for signal interpretation, together with a paper-based analytical device (PAD) utilizing filter paper as a substrate and correction fluid as a barrier for creation of detection zones. This smart platform showed excellent sensitivity with a limit of detection down to 0.12 nmol/zone, and it could be used for in-site determination of NFZ, especially for the limited resources laboratories.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: NFZ (1,2-naphthoquinone 2-semicarbazone) is an antihemorrhagic drug with adrenochrome-resembling activity. It acts by decreasing the typical bleeding time via its immediate action on capillaries and reduces blood loss. However, it is safe, and its intake is not connected to general systemic adverse effects related to sympathomimetic drugs, such as tachycardia, elevated blood pressure, or anxiety [1]. Furthermore, it has some additional effects at the cellular level, where it has venotonic and capillary stabilizing actions, thus, it is used for the management of diabetic retinopathy, varicose veins, and lower limb’s venous insufficiency [2]. Moreover, some studies suggested the efficacy of NFZ as an antiparkinsonian and antidyskinetic drug in humans by reducing the release of glutamate from cerebellar synaptosomes and lowering the level of glutamate in cerebrospinal fluid [3]. The reported analytical literature for the determination of NFZ depends mainly on voltammetric [4–6] and chromatographic techniques [7–9], in addition to one spectrophotometric method [10] and one fluorimetry method [11]. Generally, chromatographic techniques need a long time for preparations, equilibration, and analysis, and expensive mass detection or low-sensitivity UV detection [12]. In addition, they need expensive solvents. Also, the voltammetric methods for NFZ [4–6] have poor sample throughput, which makes them time-consuming. Moreover, the spectrophotometry and TLC methods [7,10] usually lack reasonable selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, the fluorimetric method [11] published for the determination of NFZ depends on reduction with sodium borohydride at 80 °C for 60 min. Thus, it is necessary to build up a new straightforward, rapid, eco-friendly, and sensitive fluorimetry method for NFZ. Fluorescence techniques occupy a key position in the framework of analytical methods for drug analysis by virtue of their high sensitivity, selectivity, cost-efficiency, and rapidness relative to other techniques like chromatography and spectrophotometry [13,14].

Due to the inherent sensitivity and selectivity of these approaches, our research group is concentrating on creating new luminescence methods for the investigation of chemicals of medicinal interest in various matrices [15–18]. For this aim, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been utilized in this study as a fluorescent sensor for the determination of NFZ by inner-filter effect (IFE)-based quenching. GQDs demonstrated a blue luminescence with a maximum λemission at 440 nm, and λexcitation at 350 nm. Being a quinonimine-based compound, NFZ shows UV absorption in the UV–visible region; thus, it can quench the fluorescence of GQDs via the inner filter effect (IFE) (Fig. 1 ). The IFE-based probe excludes any need for modification or functionalization of GQDs, offering significant flexibility and simplicity for sensitive, rapid, and accurate determination of NFZ. Furthermore, GQDs are characterized by low toxicity, safety, biocompatibility, water solubility, and photostability [19]. These features meet the increasing global demands for safe, affordable, and eco-benign analytical methods that consume very limited amounts of chemicals and energy and produce a minimum quantity of green waste. Thus, GQDs founds several applications as a fluorescence sensor for the determination of organic/inorganic molecules, metals, and biological substances [17,20,21].

In the last two decades, there have been many efforts from scientists to build reliable, sensitive, easily operated, and portable analytical devices as substitutes for expensive benchtop instruments to empower and support drug development and manufacturing processes, especially in developing countries [17,21,22,23]. These efforts are encouraged by the FDA to commercialize handheld portable devices [24]. The classical lab-based analysis by LC, spectroscopy, electrochemical, or other techniques needs bulky and expensive instruments, trained operators, multi-step procedures, and plentiful amounts of chemicals and solvents. The PADs have a substantial effect on academia and industry by virtue of their distinguished characteristics. PADs are biodegradable, cheap, and lightweight. Furthermore, cellulose, the base material of filter paper, is characterized by its hydrophilicity and network structure that offers a large surface area and permits fluid flow by capillary action. Thus, the PAD is counted to be a promising alternative for expensive analytical techniques in developing countries due to its low price and user-friendliness [25]. Thus, the development of non-expensive, simple, and easy-to-fabricate and use devices is vastly demanding, especially for low-resource laboratories. Currently, recent applications were reported for the use of PADs-smartphone combination for the analysis of different analytes, such as pharmaceutical compounds [17,21,23,26] and metal ions [27,28].

Herein, two methods have been developed for NFZ based on quenching the intense fluorescence of GQDs, adopting fluorimetry and smartphone-based platforms. We made up a smartphone-based reader for imaging and interpreting the fluorescence into analytical signals using cheap and available materials. A strong carton box lined with dark origami papers was used as the imaging box. We used a small USP-rechargeable 365 nm flashlight as an excitation source. The flashlight is multifunctional, widely commercialized, and easily obtained at an affordable price not exceeding 20 $. The captured images are analyzed by the smartphone using the free software RGB color detector. Fig. 2 represents a simple illustration of the implemented device. The real applications of the developed method are versatile, including drug determination, quality control in pharmaceutical formulations, and an assay of NFZ remainders at the exteriors of the manufacturing apparatus.

The fluorescence was recorded at room temperature utilizing a Jasco FP-6600 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) with 1 cm cell, sensitivity of 750 V, 500 nm/min scan speed, and excitation and emission monochromators slit widths of 5 and 10 nm, respectively. Scanning of the ultraviolet–visible absorption and FT/IR spectra were measured using a Jasco V-660 and Jasco FT/IR-4200 spectrophotometer, respectively. A nano ZS Zetasizer from Worcestershire, UK, was used for the determination of the zeta potential of the nanomaterial.

A smartphone-based reader for imaging and interpreting the fluorescence into analytical signals have been implemented (Fig. 2) using a box made of carton lined with dark origami papers as an imaging box. Two holes were made on the top of the box. A smartphone adopter that fits a Galaxy 53x smartphone was fixed on the first hole for capturing images. A small USP-rechargeable 365 nm flashlight was fixed in the second hole as the excitation light source. The captured images are analyzed by the smartphone using the free software RGB color detector.

GQD, 1 mg/mL aqueous suspension, was bought from Sigma Aldrich, Missouri, USA. 10.0 µg/mL GQD suspension was prepared by 1:100 v/v dilution with water. NFZ (Assay 99.9%) pure powder was obtained as a gift from Alkan Pharma Co. (6th of October City, Egypt). Milli-Q water was used in the entire study. Etioven, a 30 mg Tablet, was a product of Sanofi Aventis Co., Ltd. (France).
A standard solution of NFZ (200.0 µg/mL) was prepared in methanol. 10.0 µg/mL working solution was made by diluting the standard solution with water.

In a series of 10 mL volumetric flasks, GQD (500 µL, 10.0 µg/mL) was blended with increasing concentrations of NFZ. Water was added to the mixtures to complete the solution's final volume and then carefully blended. The prepared solutions' fluorescence intensities (F) were determined at λem 440 nm using λex 350 nm. The blank solution's fluorescence intensity (F0) was measured instantaneously using GQD without the drug. The calibration curve was created by scheming Ln (F0/F) against drug concentration [µM], and the regression equation was obtained.

The weight of ten tablets was determined, and the tablets were finely triturated. An amount of the powder equivalent to 100 mg of NFZ was weighed and put in a 100 mL volumetric flask. The final volume was achieved using methanol; the mixture was sonicated for 0.5 h and then filtered. Three increasing concentrations of this drug were put into a group of volumetric flasks (10 mL), then analyzed as explained in section “2.3”, and the %recovery was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Technique for the determination of drug remainders on apparatus surface
ce:section.ce:para: Stainless steel plates (5 × 5 cm) were cleaned with methanol and allowed to dry, then spiked with NFZ and permitted to dry at ambient conditions. The tops of cotton buds were repeatedly washed with methanol and permitted to become dry at ambient conditions. Then the cotton buds were wet with methanol and used to carefully clean the whole stainless-steel surface in vertical, then horizontal directions beginning from the outer to the inner sides. This step was repeated twice using a new bud. The heads of the buds were cut off with a scissor and collected in a clean beaker containing 5 mL methanol. The beaker containing the cotton buds’ heads was carefully wrapped and sonicated for 10 min. The cotton buds’ heads were pressed and eliminated from the solution. The solution was passed to a 10 mL volumetric flask, and the beaker was rinsed with 2 mL water and then passed to the same flask. 500 µL of GQD solution was added, and the final volume was completed with water. The solutions were mixed and then filtered with PTFE Syringe Filters (0.45 µm). A control experiment was performed at the same time by using methanol as a substitute for the drug solution. The fluorescence of the solutions was recorded at λex/λem of 350/440 nm, and the %recoveries were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based device construction and testing its linearity for NFZ determination by image processing.
ce:section.ce:para: A filter paper was cut into a square form, and Microsoft PowerPoint was used to design the detection area pattern as a circle of circles (as illustrated in Fig. 2), which was then printed and used as a model under the filter paper. A correction pen was used to draw the hydrophobic barrier based on the designed shape, and then the paper was left to dry. Each circle was made to have an inner and outer diameter of 0.8 and 1.2 cm, respectively, and a barrier thickness of 0.2 cm. A 15 µL solution of the GQDs with a concentration of 100 µg/mL was added to each detection zone and dried at room temperature for about 10 min. The PADs can be used immediately after drying or can be stored in a plastic container without any change in the signal from the GQDs for up to 3 days. Next, 20 µL of NFZ solutions (in the range of 0.4–30.0 nmol/zone) were put into the chip’s circular zones. Afterward, the chips were left to completely dry at room temperature for about 20 min. Then, a perforated black origami paper was used to cover the paper chip in a way that the perforations exactly fit the detection zones. Then, they were moved to a fixed tray inside the imaging box described previously in section 2.1. The UV torch was turned on and acted as the excitation source, and the smartphone acted as a detector and was used to record images of the PAD. The experiment was performed in triplicates. The images were analyzed using the Android RGB color detector software. The green values (G) for each detection area were measured by the software and were used as the analytical signal. The results obtained were constant upon repetitive measurement of the same prepared PAD for three successive days. A calibration curve was constructed for NFZ by plotting G0/G versus NFZ concentration (0.4–30.0 nmol/zone), where G0 and G are the G values for blank and samples, respectively. Then, the linear calibration equation was calculated.

The spectral properties and the functional groups at the surface of GQDs' were examined with different spectroscopy techniques. The GQDs' FT/IR spectrum (Fig. S1a, supplementary material) reveals a strong, broad stretching band at 3362 cm−1 that is allocated to (–OH). Also, the two bands at 1057 and 1392 cm−1 are allocated to C-OH stretching and –OH bending of the hydroxyl group, respectively. The C-O group is evidenced by its stretching and vibrational bands at 2338 and 1229 cm−1, respectively. It is concluded that the GQDs exhibit surface-OH groups that enhance their hydrophilicity and aqueous solubility and stability. Meanwhile, the band at 1634 cm−1 recognized the stretching of the aromatic core C = C. The bands at 2918 and 2985 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching of the C–H alkane group [29].

The GQDs absorption spectrum (Fig. S1b, supplementary material)) reveals two λmax at 240 and 350 nm corresponding to C = C bonds π–π* transition of the aromatic core and the oxygen-containing surface groups n–π* transition, respectively [19]. The GQDs Zeta potential was measured (−22.0 ± 3.5, n = 100) (Fig. S1c, supplementary material), where the negative Zeta potential is ascribed to –OH groups at the surface of GQDs. These negative surface charges help keep the stability of GQDs solution since their repulsion prevents their accumulation.

The GQDs have a powerful emission at 440 nm with λexcitation of 350 nm (Fig. S1b, supplementary material). Maximum fluorescence emission (λem) of GQDs is independent of the excitation wavelength λex (Fig. S1d, supplementary material), which is attributed to the fact that the used GQDs particle size is uniform with a less defeated surface. The fluorescence of GQDs depends on the pH of the medium, showing the highest fluorescence at pH ranging from 5.5 to 9.0 (Fig. S1e, supplementary material). The fluorescence declines strongly in acidic solution because the –OH groups are unionized, thus agglomerated, causing fluorescence decline. On the other hand, at pH range from 5.5 to 9, the –OH groups are ionized (-O-), conveying negative charges to the GQDs surfaces, maintaining their stability and keeping them highly fluorescent [19]. Transmission electron microscopy has been also used to investigate the morphology of the GQDs. As demonstrated in Fig. S1f (supplementary material), the GQDs are well-dispersed spherical shaped.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the fluorescence sensor and detection mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon nanomaterials attracted vast attention and found various applications as a sensitive fluorescence sensor for many analytes [17,21,23,30–32].

In this study, the fluorescence of GQDs was effectively quenched by adding NFZ (Fig. 3 ). A sequence of experiments was done to understand the source of fluorescence quenching. There are several possible mechanisms, including static and dynamic quenching, Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), and IFE [33].

NFZ has a UV-absorption peak with a strong overlap with the excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs (Fig. 4 a). So, there is a possibility of IFE or FRET mechanism. IFE comes into effect if the absorption spectrum of the quencher coincides with the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorophore or both leading to the absorption of such radiation by the quencher. FRET may take place only if the quencher’s absorption spectrum coincides with the fluorescent compound emission peak permitting the transfer of energy between them [33].
The observed fluorescence of GQDs-NFZ (Fobs) was corrected to remove IFE contribution in fluorescence quenching by applying Eq.1 [34]:

Fcorr = Fobs × antilog (
A
ex
+
A
em
2
) (1)
{Fcorr = the corrected F after exclusion of IFE, Aex is the absorbance of NFZ at the λex of GQDs, while Aem is the NFZ absorbance at λem.
Then, the fluorescence repression efficacy (%E) was computed using Eq. 2:

The curve of %E against the concentration of NFZ [NFZ] was figured out. Fig. 4b demonstrates that the IFE mechanism is the main cause of fluorescence quenching since 100% of the repression efficacy is dropped by subtracting it.

It is well known that raising the temperature results in improving the dynamic quenching and diminishing the static quenching, while it has no effect on IFE. For verifying the IFE, the Stern-Volmer study of the fluorescence repression was performed at 20, 30, and 40 °C by utilizing the following equation [33]:

Fig. 4c suggests that the Stern-Volmer correlation is linear, yet the slopes and the K (quenching constant) values are not depending on temperature, as illustrated in Table S1 (supplementary material). This ensures that either static or dynamic quenching contributes to the quenching process.

IFE is a non-radiation energy transformation procedure that occurs by reduction of the excitation source or quenching of the emitted fluorescence [34], and no new compound is formed. Thus, the absorption peak of GQDs would not alter in the presence of NFZ [33]. Fig. 4d demonstrates that the GQDs absorption spectrum drawn using a reference solution composed of water is the same as the absorption spectrum of GQDs-NFZ utilizing NFZ in the reference cell. This confirmed that no new compound was formed.

Furthermore, NFZ is a zwitterion having two pka values of 11.64 and −1.2 [35]; thus, it is predictable to be neutral at pH 7.0. The ionization (%) of the acidic and alkaline groups of NFZ was determined and found to be 99.99% for both [36], indicating an overall neutrality. This is another clue that excludes the contribution of static or dynamic quenching mechanisms in quenching of GQDs fluorescence by NFZ since static quenching requires approaching of quencher and fluorophore by electrostatic attraction while dynamic quenching involves electrostatic repulsion. Thus, the charge of NFZ is not a factor that supports either mechanism.
Thus, it is eventually concluded that the quenching mechanism is not dynamic or static but IFE. Fig. 1 illustrates the principle and mechanism of the developed probe.

The best conditions for the sensitive determination of NFZ were studied. The impact of pH on F0/F has been investigated over pH 2.0–11.5. The pH has no strong impact on F0/F; thus, this study was carried out by omitting any buffer. Additionally, changing GQDs concentration from 0.2 to 0.6 µg/mL has no influence on F0/F. 0.5 µg/mL GQDs was chosen as the optimal in this study, showing a high fluorescence intensity that allows a wide dynamic range. Additionally, some solvents, including water and short-chain alcohols, were studied as dilutants for the sensing system. No significant change in the value of F0/F was observed. Thus, water was chosen for safety, feasibility, and cost-efficiency reasons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the nanoprobe for determination of NFZ
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiency of fluorescence quenching, presented as F0/F, calculated in the presence of NFZ, is dependent on the concentration of the drugs; thus, it is suitable to be used for their quantitative determination. Fig. 3 shows the fluorescence spectra of GQD alone and in the presence of increasing concentrations of NFZ. Suppressed efficiency (%E) of 94.52% has been achieved at a final concentration of 186.0 µM of NFZ.

As clear from Fig. 4a, NFZ acts by absorbing the excitation and emission radiation of GQDs; thus, it quenches the fluorescence through primary (pIFE) and secondary (sIFE) IFE, respectively [33]. The molar absorption coefficient (ε) was calculated for NFZ at the excitation and emission wavelengths. We found that NFZ has ε350 = 6.9 × 103 and ε440 = 6.6 × 103 L/mol/cm, respectively. The conversion of the UV-absorption signal of the drug into a fluorescence signal is a smart strategy for achieving excellently enhanced sensitivity. The sensitivity obtained by adopting the developed fluorescence approach is about 8 ∼ 50 times the sensitivity achieved by measuring the absorption signal of the drug at its optimum λmax [10].

Different parameters were carefully investigated to validate the developed method for the determination of NFZ, including linearity, range, the limit of detection (LOD), the limit of quantification (LOQ), accuracy, precision, specificity, and robustness. The validation study was done as recommended by the ICH guidance (Q2 R1), which is designed for analytical procedures validation [37].

Utilizing the experimental results to assess the relationship between F0/F and drug concentrations revealed exponential behavior over the range of 0.46–186.0 µM for NFZ. This performance is known to be associated with IFE [38]. On the other hand, a linear correlation was obtained over the concentration range of 0.46–46 µM (Fig. 5 a). Instead, plotting of Ln F0/F versus concentrations gave rise to an excellent linear relationship over a wide dynamic concentration range of 0.46–186.0 µM of NFZ, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. The regression equation and the corresponding correlation coefficient are presented in Table 1 .

The lowest concentrations of NFZ that could be detected (LOD) and quantified (LOQ) were calculated based on the following equations [37]:
LOD = 3.3 SDB/SlopeR(4)
LOQ = 10 SDB/SlopeR(5)
Where SDB is the standard deviation of the blank measurement, and SlopeR is the slope of the regression line. Table 1 collects the obtained results.

To prove the specificity of the developed fluorimetry method, the probable interference from some coexisting substances has been studied. As the concentrations of the studied interferent were more than 100 times that of the drug, no interference was detected (Figure S2, supplementary material). The specificity of the PAD-smartphone platform was also confirmed by examining the influence of different possibly interfering species on the analytical signal. No interference has been observed from any of the studied substances, including glycine, starch, glucose, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl-, and SO4 2-. Thus, the determination of NFZ by the two developed methods is not affected by the commonly existing inactive ingredients confirming the suitability of the nanoprobe for the selective determination of NFZ.

The results of the developed method were compared with those that resulted from adopting a reference HPLC-UV method [8] for examining method accuracy. The reference HPLC method utilized a phenyl column and a mobile phase composed of methanol-0.02 M sodium dihydrogen phosphate mixture (60:40, v/v) of pH 6.0, with UV detection at 270 nm. Three concentrations of NFZ covering the entire range were assayed in triplicate, and the %recovery and standard deviation (SD) were calculated. Student’s t-test and Variance ratio F-test were applied to compare the two methods, and no significant difference was found since the calculated values of t and F were less than the reference values [39]. Table 2 illustrates the obtained results.

The intra-day precision of the developed method was ascertained by triplicate analysis of three concentrations of each drug within the same day. As well, repeated analysis of three concentrations of the studied drugs on three consecutive days was adopted to investigate the inter-day precision of the method [37]. The values of SD and relative standard deviation (RSD) for the two types of precision were ≤ 4.57% and ≤ 4.64%, respectively (Table 1), which revealed the good precision character of the developed method.

The developed method adopted a procedure that involved minimal steps and reagents. Thus, the errors introduced due to multi-step practice are negligible herein. The intended change in the concentration of GQDs (0.5 ± 02 µg/mL) did not produce any significant change in the analytical responses. Additionally, the method showed excellent robustness towards analytical solutions stability. Negligible variation in the analytical response was observed upon using aged (7 days) and fresh solutions of either GQD or the investigated drug. The results of the robustness study (Table 1) clearly annotate the sustained validity and reliability of the proposed method under variable experimental conditions.

To demonstrate the applications of the developed method, we applied it to the analysis of NFZ in its commercial pharmaceutical tablets. Three concentrations of NFZ were determined in triplicate and the %recoveries ± SD were calculated. The obtained results showed excellent recoveries (98.4 – 101.6%) and good agreement with the results given by the comparison HPLC-UV methods [8] since the results of t- and F- tests displayed non-significant differences [39] (Table 2). This outcome ensured the applicability of the described technique for the determination of NFZ in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of drug residues on manufacturing device surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: The method's efficiency, fastness, sensitivity, selectivity, and high throughput encouraged us to investigate its relevance for the determination of NFZ remainders on surface of fabrication device. The removal of drug residues from the apparatus employed for industrial aims is a crucial phase for good manufacturing practice in order to prevent pharmaceutics’ formulations from cross-contamination. NFZ could cause clinical drug-drug interactions if its residuals remained on the equipment surface, which could lead to cross-contamination of the next manufactured formulations of other drugs. Besides, NFZ has poor water solubility, which makes its cleaning process from equipment surfaces a tedious process [40]. Besides, computational evaluation of the ADMET (Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, toxicity) properties of NFZ revealed different aspects that possibly affect human health on long-term exposure to traces of NFZ via cross-contamination. ADMET prediction via admetSAR software [41] showed that NFZ has potential organ toxicity as a hepatotoxic compound. It also has environmental toxicity shown by fish aquatic toxicity and Tetrahymena pyriformis models. Furthermore, NFZ genomic toxicity has been established by the micronucleus assay and Ames mutagenesis test models. Furthermore, the admetSAR software showed that NFZ is classified as an endocrine-disrupting compound since it can bind thyroid, glucocorticoid, and estrogen receptors.

NFZ has also been demonstrated to be a CYP1A2 and CYP2C19 inhibitor. NFZ is also classified as an inhibitor of the hepatic transport proteins organic anion-transporting polypeptide OATP1B1 and OATP1B3. These two properties can result in clinically relevant drug-drug interactions.

Hence there is a strong demand for an efficient and rapid technique for trace detection of NFZ on the apparatus surface to validate the cleansing method [42] to prevent cross-contamination of the next batches. Previously reported methods for NFZ were never applied or suited for residual determination of NFZ. Hence, the developed method was tested for trace detection of NFZ on a stainless-steel surface to guarantee cleaning competence to fulfill this gap in the literature.

The procedure defined by Klinkenberg et al. [43] was adopted for the determination of drug remains on apparatus surfaces. The swab sampling technique was used in accordance with the FDA [44] and USP [45] guidelines for poorly water-soluble contaminants and difficulty-accessed places. A scheme for the procedure is shown in Fig. 6 .

The recovery of NFZ from the stainless-steel surface was assessed by spiking NFZ on the surface of the plate to calculate the %recovery using the following equation:
(6)
S w a b
r e c o v e r y
( % R )
=
F o u n d
t e s t
r e s u l t
x
100
s p i k e d
c o n c e n t r a t i o n

The recovery was determined at three concentration levels (n = 3) (Table 3 ). The %recoveries were in the range of 96.3–102.2 with RSD% ≤0.45%. This is considered a good recovery value as it exceeds 80 % [46]. These results qualify the developed method to be used for inspections of cleaning processes of NFZ from equipment surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development and application of a smartphone-based reader and paper-based analytical device for rapid determination of NFZ
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed approach was extended to the determination of NFZ using a smartphone-based reader and paper-based analytical device. A standalone device was executed, implementing a UV torch as the light source, a filter paper as the sample platform, and a smartphone as the detector and image processing tool (Fig. 2). The NFZ weak water solubility mandated the use of a 60% aqueous ethanol mixture for preparing its solution, which complicates the design of detection zones on the filter paper. Thus, it was obligatory to use a hydrophobic/lipophobic barrier to confine this organic solvent-rich mixture. In this study, correction fluid dispensed from a correction pen was used as a barrier to create the detection zones since it consists of titanium dioxide and resin, that have excellent confinement properties for organic solvent-rich mixtures [23,47].

Hence, a correction pen, a plotter for lipophobic/hydrophobic barriers, and the GQDs, a fluorescence turn-off sensor for recognition of NFZ, were used in combination with a smartphone reader fitted with a cheap flashlight (365 nm) as an excitation source, and a free image processing software. The paper-based chips were prepared by a freehand drawing with a correction pen on a filter paper which was put above printed patterns as described previously in the experimental section. Using the described device, a fast, cost-effective, and trustworthy way for detection of the fluorescence quenching of GQDs upon addition of NFZ was afforded in a short time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the developed method with the reported analytical literature
ce:section.ce:para: A comparison between the performance of the developed method and the analytical literature published for NFZ highlights its superiority and dominance in many aspects. The reported techniques for NFZ depend on chromatography [7–9] that require many steps and lengthy time for mobile phase preparation and column equilibration/regeneration, in addition to its needs from costly solvents and generation of plenty amount of waste. Also, the literature includes time-consuming voltammetric methods [4–6] with low sample throughput. At the same time, the spectrophotometric method [10] lacks the required sensitivity and selectivity. Herein, we developed the second fluorescence-based method for NFZ with comparable sensitivity to the first one that depended on NFZ reduction [11]; however, it entailed many disadvantages, including the use of high temperature and the need for one hour for reaction completion.

The sensitivity of our newly developed method was compared with that of those in the literature, and the data are summarized in Table 5 . Our method was 3 and 8 to 50 times more sensitive than the HPLC-UV [8] and colorimetric methods [10], respectively. Besides, our method showed similar sensitivity to the voltammetric and TLC methods for NFZ [5,7]. On the other hand, our method showed lower sensitivity than the voltammetric stripping method [4] and the LC-tandem mass methods [9]. However, these methods suffer from the limited availability of their required instrumentation in most laboratories due to their high cost and their complex use. The readily available nanoprobe and instrumentation and simple analytical procedure are great cons for the developed method that made it a great option for NFZ analysis and quality control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of methods’ greenness and eco-impact
ce:section.ce:para: The environmental effects of the developed methods have been explored by applying the Analytical GREEnness calculator (AGREE), which is a direct software that adopts multicriteria decision analysis to give interpretable results [48]. This metric evaluates sample treatment, sample size, energy and reagents consumption, miniaturization, derivatization, waste generation, number of analytes per run, and safety of reagents. The AGREE tool has been applied to assess the two developed approaches: fluorimetry and smartphone-based PAD techniques. As demonstrated in Fig. 8 , the two methods show green characters, yet the analytical greenness score of the smartphone-based method is greater than that of the fluorimetry method. This is attributed to the smaller sample size, the possibility of analysis of multiple samples / PAD, lower energy consumption, and miniaturization. These results confirmed the safety, greenness, and eco-benignness of the developed methods.

Sensitive quantitative determination of NFZ has been accomplished using GQDs nano sensor via fluorescence quenching approach. NFZ effectively quenches the fluorescence of GQDs by the IFE mechanism. The developed method has been successfully applied for the determination of NFZ in commercial tablets with excellent %recovery (98.4 – 101.6%), as well as for sensitive, rapid, and high throughput determination of NFZ remainders on the industrial machines surfaces to validate cleaning process as a crucial step for good manufacturing practice. Furthermore, the proposed approach has been tailored to design a PAD for rapid, cheap, and on-site determination of NFZ. For this aim, correction fluid has been applied as a highly resistant effective barrier for executing the PAD. The PAD has been integrated with a simple, cost-efficient, and portable smartphone-based reader for implementing a standalone device capable of fast, accurate, and easy determination of NFZ with no need for laboratory-based equipment.
This research received funding from the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, under grant No. (G: 506–247-1442).

Lateefa A. Al-Khateeb: Resources, Funding acquisition, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mahmoud El-Maghrabey: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Rania El-Shaheny: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research (DSR), King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, under grant No. (G: 506-247-1442). The authors, therefore, acknowledge with thanks DSR for the financial support provided.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123109.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
